The term ʿilm (علم) in Islamic intellectual tradition refers to knowledge in a broad sense, encompassing both revealed and rational sciences. Al-Ghazālī distinguished between ẓāhir (exoteric) and bāṭin (esoteric) dimensions of knowledge, arguing that the science of the heart (ʿilm al-qalb) must accompany formal jurisprudence. The Shāfiʿī school (al-madhhab al-Shāfiʿī) systematised the sources of fiqh: the Qurʾān, Ḥadīth, ijmāʿ (scholarly consensus), and qiyās (analogical reasoning).
